title: Data input
text: UKFE package includes several datasets can used used analyses. based data National River Flow Archive (NRFA). pre-processing script converts new releases NRFA Peak Flow Dataset data frames suitable use within UKFE (can found ‘inst’ folder package). UKFE updated shortly release use latest data. user can also input data. UKFE package contains five datasets. : AMPF: contains annual maximum data NRFA sites suitable pooling QMED. files years classed rejected removed. data frame three columns containing date, annual maximum peak flow NRFA gauge ID. PeakFlowData: contains catchment descriptors NRFA calculated statistics sites suitable pooling QMED. statistics include L-moments, L-moment ratios, sample size QMED, derived AMAX data rejected years removed. possible user temporarily edit data frame. ThamesPQ: contains daily flow catchment rainfall Thames Kingston catchment 2000-10-01 2015-09-30. three columns containing date (Date), precipitation (P) daily mean flow (Q). Dates format YYYY-MM-DD, following ISO 8601 international standard. data NRFA (gauge 39001). UKOutline: contains eastings northings around coastline UK. data sourced https://environment.data.gov.uk/. datasets help file can viewed typing name dataset console, can saved object view: user can also supply data use analyses using AddGauge function. adds gauge PeakFlowData dataframe use pooling analysis.

code: # Get an AMAX sample and plot AMplot(GetAM(58002)) # Extract POT flows for the Thames at Kingston (NRFA gauge 39001) and plot. Note that  # the indexing 'ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)]' is necessary because the date is in the first  # column of the 'ThamesPQ' data frame and the flow is in the third. ThamesQPOT <- POTextract(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)], thresh = 0.9, Plot = TRUE) #> [1] "Peaks per year: 1.867263" # Extract POT rainfall for the Thames at Kingston (NRFA gauge 39001) and plot. Note that # the indexing 'ThamesPQ[, 1:2]' is necessary because the date is in the first column  # of the 'ThamesPQ' data frame and the rainfall is in the second. ThamesPPOT <- POTt(ThamesPQ[, 1:2], threshold = 0.95, div = 14, Plot = TRUE) # Plot the 2013 water year precipitation and discharge for the Thames at Kingston  # (NRFA gauge 39001), adjusting the closeness of the precipitation and discharge  # on the y-axis to 1.8. HydroPlot(ThamesPQ, From = "2013-10-01", To = "2014-09-30", adj.y = 1.8) # Calculate the mean flows for each month for the Thames at Kingston (NRFA gauge 39001) # and set the plot title using the 'main' argument QMonThames <- MonthlyStats(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)], Stat = mean, ylab = "Discharge (m3/s)",                             main = "Thames at Kingston monthly mean flow", Plot = TRUE) # Calculate the monthly sums of rainfall for the Thames at Kingston and set the plot  # title using the 'main' argument PMonThames <- MonthlyStats(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 2)], Stat = sum, ylab = "Rainfall (mm)",                             main = "Thames at Kingston monthly rainfall", Plot = TRUE) # Calculate the BFI from daily discharge at Kingston upon Thames (which is in the  # third column of the 'ThamesPQ' data) BFI(ThamesPQ[, 3]) #> [1] 0.5814248 # Extract a wet six-month period at the Thames at Kingston (NRFA gauge 39001) during  # the 2006-2007 water year (flow is in the third column of ThamesPQ) ThamesQ <- subset(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)], Date >= "2006-11-04" & Date <= "2007-05-06") # Apply the flow split with default settings QSplit <- FlowSplit(ThamesQ$Q) # Extract a design hydrograph from the Thames at Kingston (NRFA gauge 39001) daily  # mean flow and print the resulting hydrograph ThamesDesHydro <- DesHydro(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)], EventSep = 10, N = 10) # Plot a flow duration curve for the Thames at Kingston (NRFA gauge 39001) using  # data from Oct 2000 to Sep 2015 FlowDurationCurve(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)]) # Make up some flow (Q) and stage data to act as gaugings Q <- c(177.685, 240.898, 221.954, 205.55, 383.051, 154.061, 216.582) Stage <- c(1.855, 2.109, 2.037, 1.972, 2.574, 1.748, 2.016) Observations <- data.frame(Q, Stage)  # Apply the rating function Rating(Observations) #> $Parameters #> [1] 5.6020901 0.9504756 3.3562578 #>  #> $`Discharge as a function of stage` #> [1] "Q = 5.602(h+0.95)^3.356" #>  #> $`Stage as a function of discharge` #> [1] "h = ((Q/5.602)^(1/3.356))-0.95" # Extract 15-minute rainfall from the St Ives (Cambridgeshire) rain gauge StIves <- GetDataEA_Rain(WISKI_ID = "179365", Period = "15Mins", From = "2022-01-01", To = "2025-01-31")  # Apply the DDF function. DDFExtract(StIves) #>    Hrs   X2   X5  X10  X20   X50  X100  X200  X500 X1000 #> 1    1  8.5 12.5 15.4 18.4  23.1  27.4  32.9  43.1  54.4 #> 2    3 14.9 22.0 27.0 32.3  40.5  48.2  57.9  75.8  95.6 #> 3    6 18.5 27.3 33.5 40.2  50.4  59.9  71.9  94.2 118.8 #> 4   12 22.5 33.2 40.8 48.8  61.2  72.8  87.5 114.5 144.4 #> 5   24 27.2 39.9 49.1 58.8  73.7  87.7 105.3 137.9 173.9 #> 6   36 30.2 44.4 54.6 65.4  82.0  97.6 117.2 153.4 193.5 #> 7   48 32.6 47.9 58.9 70.5  88.4 105.2 126.3 165.4 208.6 #> 8   60 34.5 50.7 62.4 74.8  93.7 111.4 133.8 175.2 221.0 #> 9   72 36.2 53.2 65.4 78.4  98.2 116.8 140.3 183.7 231.7 #> 10  84 37.6 55.4 68.1 81.6 102.2 121.6 146.0 191.2 241.2 #> 11  96 39.0 57.3 70.4 84.4 105.8 125.8 151.2 197.9 249.6

what: Derive a design hydrograph using the ReFH method
title: ReFH analysis
text: following example,  ReFH() function used derive design hydrograph, .e., hydrograph shape scaled design flood estimate (assuming site ungauged). default use  ReFH() catchment descriptors provides rainfall-runoff outputs estimate two-year design rainfall event:  output printed console list two elements. first element data frame parameters, settings, initial conditions, catchment area, peakflow. second data frame following columns: Time, Rain, NetRain, Runoff, Baseflow TotalFlow. runoff hydrograph plot also produced.

title: ReFH analysis
text: scale hydrograph design flow estimate, results ungauged pooling can used (assuming ungauged case). can use estimate FEH statistical analysis vignette: results form list. first element provides return levels, 100-year flow estimate 8th row second column. ’ll make object use scale hydrograph:
code: # Create an ungauged pooling group for site 55002  PoolUG.55002 <- Pool(CDs.55002, exclude = 55002)  # Estimate QMED for site 55002 using catchment descriptors and two donor gauges CDsQmed.55002 <- QMED(CDs.55002, DonorIDs = c(55007, 55016))  # Estimate design flows using the above pooling group and QMED Results55002 <- PoolEst(PoolUG.55002, QMED = CDsQmed.55002, CDs = CDs.55002) # Extract the 100-year flow estimate (the [[1]] denotes the first element of the list - which is a dataframe, then [8,2] is the 8th row and 2nd column of that dataframe) Q100.55002 <- Results55002[[1]][8, 2]  # Obtain the final flow outputs of the ReFH model from catchment descriptors for a default 2-year  # rainfall TotalFlow <- ReFHResult55002[[2]]$TotalFlow  #Scale this by dividing by the maximum of the flow ScaledFlow <- TotalFlow / max(TotalFlow)  #Multiply the scaled flow by our peak flow estimate and plot plot(ScaledFlow * Q100.55002, type = "l", lwd = 2, ylab = "Discharge (m3/s)", xlab = "Time (hours)")

title: Aggregate a time series — AggMonDayHour
code: # Create a data frame with a normally distributed variable at # a 15 minute sampling rate ts_seq <- seq(   as.POSIXct("2000-01-01 00:00:00", tz = "Europe/London"),   as.POSIXct("2001-01-01 00:00:00", tz = "Europe/London"),   by = 60 * 15 ) ts_df <- data.frame(DateTime = ts_seq, Var = rnorm(length(ts_seq), 10, 2))  # Aggregate to an hourly sampling rate, taking the maximum of each hour hourly <- AggMonDayHour(ts_df, func = max, Freq = "Hourly")  # Aggregate with the mean at a daily scale daily <- AggMonDayHour(ts_df, func = mean, Freq = "Daily")

code: # We'll extract a wet six month period on the Thames during the 2006-2007 hydrological year thames_q <- subset(ThamesPQ[, c(1, 3)], Date >= "2006-11-04" & Date <= "2007-05-06")  # Then apply the flow split with default settings q_split <- FlowSplit(thames_q$Q)   # Now do it with an upper baseflow level of 100 m^3/s q_split <- FlowSplit(thames_q$Q, BaseQUpper = 100)    # Next we will get a single peaked "idealised" hydrograph using the ReFH function q_refh <- ReFH(GetCDs(15006))  q_refh <- q_refh[[2]]$TotalFlow  # Now use the function with and without an upward adjustment of the baseflow on the falling limb q_flow_split <- FlowSplit(q_refh)  q_flow_split <- FlowSplit(q_refh, AdjUp = 0.15)

what: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
text: compares RMSE four distribution fits single AMAX sample.

title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
code: GoFCompare(x)

title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
text: x numeric vector (AMAX sample)

title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
text: list. first element dataframe four columns one row results. column standardised RMSE associated one four distributions (GEV, GenLog, Gumbel, Kappa3). second element character string stating distribution best fit.

title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
text: function calculates RMSE fit score four distributions (GEV, GenLog, Gumbel, & Kappa3). lowest RMSE best fit. works follows. distribution: Step1. Simulate 500 samples size x. Step2. Calculate mean across 500 samples rank create ordered central estimate. Step3. Calculate RMSE result step 2 ordered x. Step4. Standardise RMSE dividing mean x multiply 100 (RMSE percentage mean). Note hypothesis test. comparing fit across distributions.

title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
text: Anthony Hammond

title: Goodness of fit comparison (single sample) — GoFCompare
code: # Get an AMAX sample and then compare the fit am_15006 <- GetAM(15006) GoFCompare(am_15006$Flow) #> [[1]] #>     GEV GenLog Gumbel Kappa3 #> 1 3.452  3.302  3.381  3.189 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "Kappa3 has the best fit" #>

title: Heterogeneity measure (H2) for pooling groups. — H2

title: Heterogeneity measure (H2) for pooling groups. — H2
text: vector two characters; first representing H2 score second stating qualitative measure heterogeneity.

title: Heterogeneity measure (H2) for pooling groups. — H2
text: H2 measure developed Hosking & Wallis can found book 'Regional Frequency Analysis: Approach Based L-Moments' (1997). also adopted use Flood Estimation Handbook (1999) described volume 3. works recreating 500 pooling groups sample sizes, assuming four parameter Kappa distribution (parameters pooled L-moments). L-moment ratios calculated 500 simulated pooling groups. heterogeneity determined comparing variance L-moment ratios observed pooling group variance L-moment ratios across simulated pooling groups. simulations homogeneous, therefore observed pooling group homogeneous expectation variance similar average simulated variance.

title: Heterogeneity measure (H2) for pooling groups. — H2

title: Heterogeneity measure (H2) for pooling groups. — H2

title: Historic flood maximum likelihood estimation — HistoricMLE
code: # Get an annual maximum sample and assume 3 exceedances over 100 years # with a threhsold of 140m 3/s AM71011 <- GetAM(71011) HistoricMLE(AM71011$Flow, k = 3, h = 100, threshold = 140) #>           Method   Loc Scale    Shape    LCV   LSKEW #> 1  AMAX Lmoments 121.9 8.214 0.006002 0.0674 -0.0060 #> 2 Historical MLE 120.8 7.299 0.041070 0.0608 -0.0411   # Now estimate again but set Uncertainty = TRUE HistoricMLE(AM71011$Flow, k = 3, h = 100, threshold = 140, Uncertainty = TRUE) #> $`Historic Adjustment` #>           Method   Loc Scale    Shape    LCV   LSKEW #> 1  AMAX Lmoments 121.9 8.214 0.006002 0.0674 -0.0060 #> 2 Historical MLE 120.8 7.299 0.041070 0.0608 -0.0411 #>  #> $Uncertainty #>    ReturnPeriod CentralAMAX CentralHistoric FSE_AMAX FSE_Historic #> 1             2         125             123    1.012        1.011 #> 2            10         140             136    1.021        1.017 #> 3            20         146             141    1.027        1.022 #> 4            30         149             144    1.031        1.025 #> 5            50         154             147    1.036        1.028 #> 6            75         157             150    1.041        1.032 #> 7           100         159             151    1.044        1.034 #> 8           200         165             156    1.053        1.041 #> 9           500         172             161    1.067        1.050 #> 10         1000         177             165    1.077        1.057 #>

title: Trend hypothesis test — TrendTest
text: test can performed numeric vector, data.frame dates first column associated variable interest second. choice can made Mann Kendall, Pearson, Spearman tests. Spearman Mann Kendall based ranks therefore results whether dates included . default Mann Kendall.  default test trend (alternative = "two.sided"). positive trend set alternative "greater", test negative trend set alternative "less". Interpretation: testing positive trend (alternative = "greater") P_value probability exceeding observed statistic null hypothesis (less zero).  vice versa true testing negative trend (alternative = "less"). alternative = "two.sided" P_value probability exceeding absolute value observed statistic null hypothesis (different zero). Low P values indicate null hypothesis less likely.

title: Uncertainty quantification for gauged and ungauged pooled estimates — Uncertainty
text: Uncertainty gauged ungauged case quantified specifically (bespoke) pooling group according methods detailed Hammond, . (2021). Sampling uncertainty UK design flood estimation. Hydrology Research. 1357-1371. 52 (6). Note function quantifies sampling (aleatoric) uncertainty. quantify uncertainty associated models, model choices applied, hydrometric data. method assumes AMAX samples within pooling group independent serially independent identically distributed. default ungauged QMED fse 1.5. FSE QMED 2025 regression model catchments suitable QMED estimation. Gauged = TRUE, assumption top site pooling group gauged subject site. Gauged case pooling group bootstrapped (parametrically) 200 times create 200 pooling groups. PoolEst function applied 200 resampled pooling groups along 200 resampled QMED gauged subject site. FSE calculated range 200 estimates return period. ungauged case, gauge bootstrapped (parametrically) w x 200 times, w weighting gauge. growth factors associated 200 LMoment ratios calculated multiplied sampled QMED based fseQMED value. .e SampledQMED = exp(rnorm(1, log(QMEDEstimate), log(fseQMED)). FSEs calculated across 200 results return period.

title: Zdist Goodness of fit measure for pooling groups — Zdists
text: goodness fit measure provides Z-Score quantifies number standard deviations mean normal distribution. determine goodness fit given distribution (assume GEV example), 500 pooling groups formed match number sites samples sizes pooling group interest. formed simulation GEV distribution LCV LSKEW weighted mean LCV LSKEW pooling group (weighted sample size) median 1. weighted mean L-Kurtosis observed pooling group (tR4) compared mean standard deviation (sd) L-Kurtosis simulated pooling groups (tR4_Dist) calculating associated Z-score: (tR4 - mean(tR4_Dist)) / sd(tR4_Dist). fit distribution can considered acceptable absolute Z-Score less 1.645 (essentially hypothesis test alpha level equal 0.1). done candidate distributions lowest absolute score considered best fit. NOTE: slightly different zdist function described science report 'Improving FEH statistical procedures flood frequency estimation, Environment Agency (2008)'. function assumes theoretical LKurtosis function pooled LSKEW compare distribution LKurtosis simulated pooling groups. means Gumbel distribution compared (hence change recommendation 'Regional Frequency Analysis' Hosking & Wallis (1997)), .e. Gumbel distribution now included whereas previously .

title: Zdist Goodness of fit measure for pooling groups — Zdists
text: Anthony Hammond

title: Zdist Goodness of fit measure for pooling groups — Zdists
code: # Get CDs, form a pooling group, and calculate the Z-dists cds_39001 <- GetCDs(39001) pool_39001 <- Pool(cds_39001, N = 500) Zdists(pool_39001) #> [[1]] #>      GEV GenLog Gumbel Kappa3 #> 1 -0.509  -2.66  -1.14  -1.99 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "GEV has the best fit" #>

title: UKFE 2.0.0
text: FEH2025: Pool, QMED, PoolEst functions amended apply FEH2025 methods. QMED donors: QMED function amended can input vector (‘list’ normal parlance) gauge IDs donors, can use .Donors argument simply choose N donors N closest catchment centroid used. can also return details gauges associated adjustments distances etc. QuickResults function uses default 8 closest donors. Pooling uncertainty: default, uncertainty calculated pooled estimates done bespoke pooling group (rather using generalised approximating equations - previous version). done resampling pooling group assumption FSE QMED (Gauged = FALSE) – can adjusted user. uncertainty result provided form FSE return period associated peak flow estimate. Defaults: defaults Pooling QMED changed urban adjustments etc applied unless otherwise specified. Donor default choose gauge (.e. URBEXT maximum set 1), need reduce wish avoid urban donors using default ‘.Donors’ argument. CDs: new descriptors now available via GetCDs CDsXML function (CDsXML_legacy function read older ones - won’t work new Pool QMED functions need adapted). Data: Removed two dataframes QMEDData NRFAData replaced single dataframe called PeakFlowData (incorporates info sites suitable pooling /QMED. AMSP also changed. dataframe annual maximum samples. now includes sites suitable QMED well sites suitable pooling – therefore renamed AMPF. Historic flood estimation: now function called HistoricalMLE, allows estimate peak flows whilst accounting k peaks specified threshold h years prior AMAX record. present option specifying historic discharges. Seasonality Plot: Seasonality function added, seasonality plotting. pooled seasonality plot also added plots provided DiagPlots function. LowFlows: Function called LowFlows added estimates low flows catchment descriptors (individual inputs AREA, SAAR, estimate BFI). returns Qmean, Q95, Q70, Q50, Q10, Q05. ReFH: Amended ReFH function make flexible tool evaluating plausible response input rainfall.

title: UKFE 1.0.0

title: UKFE 1.0.0

Error handling: Improved Kappa3Est(), GEVPars(), LSkew(), LKurt() required arguments (x, L1, LCV, LSKEW) missing. Error messaging: informative message Pool() DeUrb = TRUE sites available. H2 method: Regional average L-moments now weighted record length. Heavy optimisation penalties applied boundaries prevent boundary solutions. Small correction applied kappa optimisation equation. fse implementation: Updated QuickResults(). Styling: Applied styler::style_pkg() align tidyverse conventions (cosmetic ). Improved accessibility readability plots across multiple functions (better colour contrast, line types, text sizing, axis labelling, descriptive titles).

title: UKFE 1.0.0

Optimisation bounds: Corrected Kappa3GF() Kappa3Pars(). Column indexing: Fixed QuickResults() selecting wrong column (now indexed name). ReFH(): Fixed calculation nearest odd multiple timestep duration unspecified. AMImport(): Adjusted updated date format. Zdists(): Corrected GEV GenLog results wrong way round.
